topic: Someone receives an apology letter years after the incident
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope, thick and cream-colored, sat accusingly on the kitchen counter. Dust motes danced in the afternoon sun slanting through the window, illuminating the crisp, formal script inside. It was from Mark, a letter penned nearly a decade ago, an apology for a careless word that had fractured everything between them. My hands trembled as I picked up the paper, the edges feeling brittle under my touch. I reread the lines, each carefully chosen sentence a tiny shard digging into my chest. A slow, hot flush crept up my neck. I dropped the letter onto the wooden table with a soft thud, the sound echoing unnervingly loud in the silence of the apartment. I walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent street, my breath catching in shallow, rapid gasps. The air felt too thin to draw in.

[story 2]

I hadn't expected the postman to bring it. It was a forgotten piece of paper, tucked between bills and junk mail, surfacing from a drawer I hadn't opened in years. When I finally unfolded it, the familiar handwriting swam before my eyes, a ghost from a past I desperately tried to seal off. A tightness constricted my throat, making swallowing a conscious, difficult effort. I crumpled the paper once, then smoothed it out again, tracing the ink with a fingertip. My jaw clenched so hard I could feel the strain in my molars. I stood by the hallway mirror, watching my reflection—a stranger whose eyes held a deep, weary sorrow. The silence of the house pressed in, heavy and suffocating, amplifying the frantic drumming of my own pulse against my ribs.

[story 3]

The letter arrived on a Tuesday, completely unannounced. I found it in the mail pile, and the sheer unexpectedness of it made my stomach clench. When I finally opened it, the familiar words washed over me, cold and sharp. I sat on the edge of the bed, the springs groaning beneath my weight, and stared at the paper as if it were a foreign object. My gaze kept flicking back and forth across the page, unable to settle. A low, involuntary sound escaped my lips—a small, choked noise that I immediately tried to cover with a cough. My fingers tightened around the edges of the stationery until the paper creased. I didn't move to put it away; I just held it, letting the weight of the past settle squarely upon my shoulders.
